title: This 100-Million-Year-Old Snake Had Legs and a Cheekbone!
summary:
Have you ever seen a snake with legs? Probably not — but 100 million years ago, some snakes had them! A fossil from Argentina called Najash rionegrina is helping scientists understand how snakes evolved.

Scientists used a special scanner (like a super-powered X-ray) to look inside the fossil without breaking it. They found something surprising: this ancient snake still had a cheekbone, called the jugal bone. Most snakes today don't have that bone at all!

"Our findings support the idea that the ancestors of modern snakes were big-bodied and big-mouthed — instead of small burrowing forms as previously thought," said Fernando Garberoglio, the lead scientist on the study.

The fossil also shows that early snakes kept their hind legs for a long time before losing them. That's like having tiny back legs for millions of years!

This discovery is super important because it changes the story of how snakes became the slithery creatures we know today. Next time you see a snake, remember: its ancient relatives might have looked very different!
why_it_matters: This discovery shows that animals can change a lot over time, and even creatures we see today had very different ancestors long ago.

keywords.explanation: The name of the ancient snake fossil found in Argentina.
The preserved remains of a living thing from long ago.
Body parts that some ancient snakes still had.
A bone called the jugal bone that this snake still had.
A special machine scientists used to look inside the fossil.

What surprising bone did this ancient snake still have?
What did scientists use to look inside the fossil?

background_read: Many millions of years ago, snakes had legs and looked more like lizards.
Fossils are like time capsules that help scientists learn about ancient animals.
The study of how animals change over time is called evolution.
Article_Structure: WHO: The ancient snake called Najash rionegrina
WHAT: Had legs and a cheekbone that modern snakes lack
WHY: It changes the story of how snakes evolved from big-bodied ancestors, not small burrowing ones

perspectives.description: They are excited because the fossil gives new clues about snake evolution.
Kids find it amazing that snakes once had legs, which sparks curiosity about evolution.
Some may question if one fossil is enough to rewrite the whole story of snake evolution.
